title: Alaska State Reps. Introduce Abortion Abolition Bill
author: James Silberman
orientation: James Silberman reports on Alaska HB178, the Abolition of Abortion in Alaska Act, introduced by Reps. David Eastman and Sharon Jackson, which would establish equal protection for preborn humans, nullify federal court rulings legalizing abortion, and repeal pro-life regulations that grant exceptions. The piece contrasts the abolitionist approach with mainstream pro-life lobbying and details the political headwinds in the Alaska House.
audiences: Pro-life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolition

questions.question: What does Alaska HB178 actually do, and who introduced it?
questions.answer: On May 15, 2019, Reps. David Eastman (R-Wasilla) and Sharon Jackson (R-Eagle River) introduced the Abolition of Abortion in Alaska Act (HB178), which makes all human beings equal under the law and provides equal protection to all, making Alaska the sixth state that year to see a total abolition bill introduced.
questions.quote: State Reps. David Eastman (R-Wasilla) and Sharon Jackson (R-Eagle River) introduced the Abolition of Abortion in Alaska Act on May 15th making Alaska the sixth state this year in which a bill of total abolition has been introduced.

questions.question: How does HB178 differ from the high-profile Alabama and Georgia abortion bills of 2019?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: Unlike Alabama's and Georgia's bills, the Alaska legislation refuses to recognize the federal courts' self-claimed authority to legalize abortion. The article frames this rejection of judicial supremacy as central to the abolitionist position.
questions.quote: Unlike the much-hyped Alabama and Georgia abortion bills, Eastman and Jackson’s does not recognize the self-appointed authority of the federal courts to legalize murder. This point is central to the abolitionist perspective.

questions.question: On what constitutional basis does HB178 declare federal abortion rulings invalid in Alaska?
questions.answer: The bill argues that any statute, regulation, rule, or order legalizing abortion violates a child's right to life under the Fourteenth Amendment, and is therefore not authorized by the Constitution, not the supreme law of the land, and null and void within Alaska.
questions.quote: [A] statute, regulation, rule, or order that has the purpose, intent, or effect of legalizing any abortion in the state infringes on a child’s right to life in violation of the Fourteenth Amendment to the Constitution of the United States and, therefore, is not made in accordance with the Constitution of the United States, is not authorized by the Constitution of the United States, is not the supreme law of the land, and, consequently, is invalid in this state and shall be considered null and void and of no effect in this state.

questions.question: Why does Eastman argue the Alaska legislature, not the courts, should define privacy rights?
questions.answer: Eastman points to the Alaska State Constitution, which assigns the legislature the duty to implement privacy rights through legislation. He argues the courts have overstepped by inventing a definition of privacy untethered from the constitution's original meaning, and that the legislature must reclaim that role and clarify that privacy does not shield the taking of innocent life.
questions.quote: Our State Constitution specifically assigns the legislature the duty of implementing privacy rights with legislation. The courts have clearly overstepped their bounds by creating a new definition of privacy that is entirely different than that for which the constitution was written. The legislature must fulfill its constitutional duty to provide the definition of privacy, and this bill begins that process by making it clear that the right to take an innocent human life is not protected by the cloak of privacy.

questions.question: How does Eastman use Alaska's marijuana stance to argue for nullifying federal abortion law?
questions.answer: In his sponsor statement, Eastman points out that Alaska already defied federal authority on the manufacture, sale, and recreational use of marijuana. He argues that if standing up to the federal government was warranted to defend recreational drug use, it is far more warranted to defend the right of preborn Alaskan children to live.
questions.quote: As a state, we have already decided to stand up to federal overreach concerning the manufacturing, sale and recreational use of marijuana. If it was important that we as a state stand up to defend the right of Alaskans to use marijuana for recreational purposes, how much more important is it that we today stand up to federal overreach when it violates the right of an innocent Alaskan child to live.

questions.question: Why does HB178 repeal even existing pro-life laws?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: The bill repeals every law that allows abortion, including pro-life regulations, on the grounds that any law that regulates abortion rather than abolishing it still infringes on the preborn person's right to life under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments.
questions.quote: The third important aspect of the Abolition of Abortion in Alaska Act is the repealing of every law allowing abortion, including pro-life ones. These pro-life laws which regulate abortion rather than abolishing it are unconstitutional, as they “infringe on a person’s right to life under the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments to the Constitution of the United States.”

questions.question: Does HB178 allow exceptions for rape, incest, or self-induced abortion?
questions.answer: No exceptions are permitted under HB178. The article notes that most pro-life laws permit killing a preborn human depending on the circumstances of conception, and uniformly grant automatic legal immunity to mothers who self-induce abortions; HB178 closes both of those carve-outs.
questions.quote: No exceptions are allowed under AK HB178. Most pro-life laws allow for murdering a preborn human being in the womb depending on the circumstances of the child’s conception and all pro-life laws provide exceptions for all self-induced abortions by [giving automatic legal immunity to the parents of the aborted child]

questions.question: What lesson does Eastman draw from Roe v. Wade about regulating abortion?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: Eastman points to the Supreme Court's reasoning in Roe, where the Court rejected Texas's claim that the unborn child has a right to life because Texas did not consistently recognize that right in its own laws (e.g., exempting mothers from prosecution). For Eastman this proves that inconsistent, regulation-based laws actively undermine the case for the child's personhood.
questions.quote: In Roe v. Wade, the State of Texas tried to argue that the child had a right to life. The Supreme Court turned around and said that Texas didn’t actually mean that because Texas did not consistently recognize and honor a child’s right to life in their own state laws, such as exempting mothers from legal consequence in the killing of the child within their womb.

questions.question: How does Eastman characterize 46 years of pro-life regulatory strategy?
questions.audience: Pro-life advocate weighing incrementalism vs. abolition
questions.answer: Eastman calls it a wandering in the wilderness, arguing that whether or not a child has a right to live, regulating abortion is a meaningless exercise. He insists that legally recognizing life from conception is the only way out.
questions.quote: Our nation has spent more than forty years lost in the wilderness of abortion regulation. If a child does not have a right to live, it is a meaningless exercise. And if a child has a right to live, it is a meaningless exercise. I am certain there are those who would advocate remaining lost in that wilderness for another forty years. Legally recognizing that life begins at conception is the only way out of that wilderness.

questions.question: What does the article say about National Right to Life's record on equal-protection legislation?
questions.answer: In the 46 years since Roe, National Right to Life has not sponsored or supported a single state-level bill making born and preborn humans equal under the law. The piece further notes that NRTL state affiliates in Indiana, Oklahoma, and Idaho have actively helped kill abolition bills.
questions.quote: In the 46 years since Roe, National Right to Life, the most prominent member of the legislation and lobbying wing of the pro-life movement, hasn’t sponsored or supported a state-level bill making born and preborn human beings equal under the law.

questions.question: How are Alaska's two anti-abortion lobbying groups split on HB178?
questions.answer: Alaska Right to Life (which despite the name is not affiliated with National Right to Life) supports the bill, while Pro-Life Alaska (which is an NRTL affiliate) opposes it. Eastman expects Pro-Life Alaska's opposition to be conducted quietly, consistent with NRTL affiliates' pattern elsewhere.
questions.quote: Alaska Right to Life supports the bill and Pro-Life Alaska opposes it. Eastman said he “anticipates that pro-life Alaska will oppose the bill quietly.” This follows the M.O. of National Right to Life-affiliated groups around the country who don’t want word getting out about their opposition to abortion’s abolition.

questions.question: Why does HB178 face an uphill battle in a Republican-majority Alaska House?
questions.answer: Although Republicans hold 23 of 40 House seats, Reps. Gary Knopp, Gabrielle LeDoux, and Louise Stutes refused to back a Republican Speaker. Knopp publicly framed a bare-minimum majority as unworkable, but the article argues he could have built a Republican-led coalition with centrist Democrats and instead chose to throw control to a Democrat-led majority.
questions.quote: The bill faces an uphill battle due to a number of Republicans defecting to caucus with Democrats despite being voted in as Republicans. The GOP holds 23 of 40 seats in the Alaska State House, yet the minority caucus are all Republicans as Gary Knopp (R-Kenai), Gabrielle LeDoux (R-Anchorage), and Louise Stutes (R-Kodiak) refused to support a Republican for Speaker of the House.

questions.question: How was the committee that received HB178 structured, and what does Eastman say about that?
questions.answer: HB178 was assigned to the House Health and Social Services Committee, which has five Democrats and two Republicans, with both co-chairs being Democrats. Eastman accuses many of his House Republican colleagues of voting to install pro-abortion Democrats over the very committees that would hear the bill, even while branding themselves as 'very pro-life.'
questions.quote: Many of the other Republicans in the House voted to put rabidly pro-abortion Democrat legislators in charge of the very committees assigned to hear the bill. Some even claimed to be ‘very pro-life’ while they were casting those votes.

questions.question: How does the article frame HB178 in moral and biblical terms?
questions.answer: Silberman closes by framing HB178 as conforming to God's commands to love your neighbor and rescue those being led to slaughter, as well as to the Constitution's commands to uphold the right to life and provide equal justice and protection.
questions.quote: Alaskans have been presented with a bill which accords with God’s commands to love your neighbor and rescue those being led to slaughter and the Constitution’s commands to uphold the right to life and provide equal justice and protection.
